q: What is Lakeflow Connect and how does it differ from standard connectors in Databricks?
a: Lakeflow Connect is Databricks' managed ingestion platform that provides high-throughput, low-latency connectivity from SaaS applications and databases into the Lakehouse. It comes in two flavors: managed connectors (fully hosted by Databricks, requiring no infrastructure management) and standard connectors (user-operated, more flexible). Managed connectors for sources like Salesforce, SQL Server, ServiceNow, and MySQL use change data capture (CDC) to ingest only changed rows on each run after the initial full load, making them highly efficient. The resulting ingestion pipeline is governed by Unity Catalog and backed by serverless compute and Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines under the hood. Standard connectors give teams more control and support a wider range of sources but require the user to operate the connector infrastructure.
fj: Lakeflow Connect is just a rebranding of COPY INTO with a UI wrapper.
fs: COPY INTO is a SQL command for idempotent batch file ingestion from cloud storage; Lakeflow Connect is a purpose-built CDC-driven ingestion service for SaaS and database sources, using serverless compute and Declarative Pipelines—it is architecturally different.

q: When would you choose Lakeflow Connect managed connectors over Auto Loader or COPY INTO for ingesting data into Databricks?
a: Choose Lakeflow Connect managed connectors when your source is a SaaS application (Salesforce, ServiceNow) or a relational database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server) where CDC-based row-level change tracking is important and you want zero infrastructure management. Choose Auto Loader when you are ingesting files landing in cloud object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS) at scale—millions of files over time—and need schema evolution support with exactly-once semantics. Choose COPY INTO when you have a smaller, well-bounded set of files (thousands) to load idempotently in batch SQL workflows. Choose partner connectors (Fivetran, Airbyte) when Lakeflow Connect does not support your source or you already have a managed ELT vendor in your stack. The key decision axes are: source type, volume/frequency, and how much governance and Unity Catalog lineage you need natively.
fj: Auto Loader is always better than Lakeflow Connect because it supports schema evolution.
fs: Auto Loader only handles files in cloud storage; it cannot connect to SaaS APIs or relational databases with CDC. Lakeflow Connect is the right tool for those sources, and it also integrates with Unity Catalog governance natively.

q: How do you ingest semi-structured JSON data with nested types into a Delta table using Auto Loader in Databricks?
a: Auto Loader's `cloudFiles` format handles JSON natively and can infer nested schema automatically. You set `cloudFiles.schemaLocation` so Databricks stores the inferred schema between runs. For nested types, Spark reads them as `StructType` or `ArrayType` columns. You can then use `from_json`, `explode`, or dot-notation to access nested fields in downstream transformations. When the source schema evolves (a new nested key appears), Auto Loader's schema evolution mode (`cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode = 'addNewColumns'`) automatically adds the new columns to the target Delta table without restarting the stream. If you need to flatten the JSON before landing it, you add a `select` with dot-notation or `inline` expressions in the same streaming query before the `writeStream` sink.
fj: You must pre-define the full JSON schema manually before using Auto Loader, otherwise it will fail.
fs: Auto Loader supports schema inference via `cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes`; you only need to provide a schema manually if you want to enforce a strict contract. Schema inference with `schemaLocation` is the recommended pattern for semi-structured sources.

code.label: Auto Loader JSON ingest with schema inference

q: What are Databricks notebook magic commands and how do widgets enable parameterized notebooks?
a: Magic commands are special directives prefixed with `%` that change the execution context or behavior of a notebook cell. `%python`, `%sql`, `%scala`, and `%r` switch the language for that cell regardless of the notebook's default language. `%md` renders a cell as Markdown documentation. `%sh` runs shell commands on the driver. `%fs` is a shortcut for `dbutils.fs` filesystem operations. `%run` executes another notebook in the same session, allowing code reuse via includes. Widgets (`dbutils.widgets`) let you add interactive input controls—text, dropdown, combobox, multiselect—to a notebook. When the notebook is called from a Databricks Job or another notebook via `%run`, widget values can be passed as parameters, making the same notebook reusable across environments (dev/prod) and date ranges without code changes.
fj: Magic commands only work in Python notebooks and cannot be used in SQL notebooks.
fs: Magic commands work in all notebook types regardless of the default language. The `%sql` magic, for example, is commonly used inside a Python notebook to run a quick SQL query in the same session.

code.label: Widget definition and retrieval
lines: # Define a text widget with default value

q: Explain the differences between temporary views, global temporary views, and permanent views in Databricks.
a: A temporary view (`CREATE TEMP VIEW`) is session-scoped and lives only for the duration of the Spark session that created it—it is invisible to other sessions and dropped automatically when the session ends. A global temporary view (`CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW`) is cluster-scoped and accessible across all sessions on the same cluster via the special database `global_temp`, but it is also dropped when the cluster restarts. A permanent view (`CREATE VIEW` in Unity Catalog) is persisted in the metastore, is accessible to any user with SELECT privilege, survives cluster restarts, and participates in Unity Catalog lineage and governance. Use temporary views for intermediate transformation steps within a single notebook. Use permanent views to expose curated datasets to downstream consumers without copying the underlying data. Global temporary views are rarely the right choice in production because their lifespan is tied to cluster availability.
fj: Global temporary views persist across cluster restarts and can be used in production as a permanent sharing mechanism.
fs: Global temporary views are dropped when the cluster is restarted. Only permanent views in Unity Catalog persist beyond a cluster lifecycle and are safe for production data sharing.

code.label: All three view types
lines: SELECT region, SUM(revenue) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY region;
CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW cluster_summary AS
SELECT region, SUM(revenue) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY region;
SELECT region, SUM(revenue) AS total FROM catalog.bronze.orders GROUP BY region;

q: What does Databricks Connect enable and when would you use it over running code directly in a workspace notebook?
a: Databricks Connect is a client library that lets you run Spark code from a local IDE (VS Code, PyCharm, IntelliJ) against a remote Databricks cluster without uploading files or using the notebook UI. It translates local PySpark or Scala API calls into remote execution on the cluster, returning results to your local process. You would use it when you want full IDE capabilities—step-through debugging, local unit tests with a real cluster, code navigation, and refactoring tools—that are unavailable in the notebook editor. It is also the right choice when your CI pipeline needs to run integration tests against a live cluster before merging code. Databricks Connect v2 (for Databricks Runtime 13+) supports the DataFrame API, Spark SQL, and UDFs, but does not support RDD APIs or Structured Streaming.
fj: Databricks Connect lets you run code entirely locally without a Databricks cluster, similar to a local PySpark installation.
fs: Databricks Connect still executes Spark operations on a remote Databricks cluster—it is not local execution. It just allows you to author and debug code from a local IDE while the computation happens in the cloud.

q: What are the key DDL and DML operations you need to know for the Databricks DE Associate exam, and what makes MERGE INTO different from a plain INSERT?
a: The core DDL operations are CREATE TABLE (with USING DELTA, LOCATION, PARTITIONED BY, and TBLPROPERTIES clauses), ALTER TABLE (add/drop columns, rename, set properties), and DROP TABLE. Key DML operations are INSERT INTO (append rows), INSERT OVERWRITE (atomically replace a partition or entire table), UPDATE (change values in matching rows), DELETE (remove matching rows), and MERGE INTO (upsert: match rows between a source and target, then apply conditional INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE in one atomic transaction). MERGE INTO differs from a plain INSERT because it handles three cases in one statement: when a row matches the key you can UPDATE or DELETE it; when there is no match you can INSERT it. This is the standard CDC pattern. Delta Lake makes MERGE ACID-compliant, meaning a failed MERGE leaves the table unchanged.
fj: INSERT OVERWRITE and MERGE INTO are interchangeable; both do upserts.
fs: INSERT OVERWRITE replaces all rows (or a partition) with new data—it is a destructive full replacement. MERGE INTO performs row-level conditional logic (match/not-match), making it the correct tool for incremental CDC upserts where only changed rows should be updated.

code.label: MERGE INTO upsert pattern
lines: UPDATE SET target.name = source.name, target.updated_at = source.updated_at
WHEN NOT MATCHED AND source.op = 'I' THEN

q: How do you write an idempotent INSERT OVERWRITE statement in Databricks and why is it preferred over DELETE + INSERT for batch loads?
a: INSERT OVERWRITE replaces the target table or a specific partition atomically in a single operation. In Delta Lake, the overwrite is transactional: the old data files are logically removed and the new data files are committed in one Delta log entry. If the job fails mid-write, the transaction is rolled back and the table remains in its previous state—there is no window where rows are partially deleted. By contrast, DELETE followed by INSERT is two separate transactions: a crash after the DELETE but before the INSERT leaves the table empty. For partition-level idempotency, set `spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode = 'dynamic'` so only the partitions present in the incoming DataFrame are replaced, leaving untouched partitions intact.
fj: You should use DELETE + INSERT because it is more explicit and easier to understand than INSERT OVERWRITE.
fs: DELETE + INSERT is not atomic across the two statements; a failure between them corrupts the table. INSERT OVERWRITE is a single atomic transaction in Delta Lake, making it the correct idempotent pattern for batch loads.

q: How do you build a Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipeline end-to-end in Python, and what are streaming tables vs. materialized views?
a: A Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipeline (formerly Delta Live Tables) is defined as a Python or SQL file where you use the `@dlt.table` decorator (or `CREATE OR REFRESH STREAMING TABLE` / `CREATE OR REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW` in SQL) to declare datasets. Streaming tables are the right choice for append-only or CDC sources that you process incrementally using Structured Streaming semantics—each run processes only new data. Materialized views recompute their result from scratch (or incrementally if Delta supports it) and are the right choice for aggregate or join outputs that derive from slowly-changing inputs. You wire datasets together by referencing upstream tables with `dlt.read()` (batch) or `dlt.read_stream()` (streaming), and Databricks infers the DAG automatically. The pipeline is deployed as a Lakeflow pipeline resource with its own compute, mode (Continuous or Triggered), and Unity Catalog target catalog/schema.
fj: In a Lakeflow pipeline, you must explicitly define the execution order of tables using a dependency list parameter.
fs: Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines infer the execution order automatically from the `dlt.read()` and `dlt.read_stream()` calls in each table definition. You never specify execution order manually.

code.label: End-to-end Lakeflow pipeline definition
lines: # Bronze: streaming table from Auto Loader

q: What are the three expectation decorators in Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines, and when do you use each one?
a: Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines provides three expectation decorators for data quality enforcement. `@dlt.expect('name', condition)` keeps all records—even those that violate the condition—but records violation metrics in the pipeline event log so you can monitor quality without discarding data. Use this for soft quality checks where you want visibility but not enforcement. `@dlt.expect_or_drop('name', condition)` drops any record that violates the condition and continues the pipeline. Use this when bad records should not pollute downstream tables but the pipeline itself should keep running. `@dlt.expect_or_fail('name', condition)` halts the entire pipeline run when any record violates the condition. Use this when data quality is a hard contract—for example, a primary key must never be NULL in a CDC stream—and processing corrupted data would cause irreversible downstream damage.
fj: `@dlt.expect_or_fail` simply skips the offending records and logs a warning, similar to expect_or_drop.
fs: `@dlt.expect_or_fail` stops the entire pipeline run with a failure status when any record violates the expectation. It does not skip records. Use `@dlt.expect_or_drop` to skip bad records while allowing the pipeline to continue.

code.label: All three expectation types
lines: # Soft check: keep bad rows, track metric

q: How do you create a multi-task Databricks Workflow (Lakeflow Job) with task dependencies, and what task types are available?
a: A Lakeflow Job (Databricks Workflow) is the orchestration primitive that lets you chain tasks into a DAG. You create a job via the UI, CLI, REST API, or Declarative Automation Bundles (YAML). Each task has a type: Notebook (runs a .ipynb or workspace notebook), Python script (runs a .py file from Repos or workspace), Python wheel (runs an installed package entry point), SQL (runs DBSQL queries or a .sql file), Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines (triggers a Spark Declarative Pipeline), JAR, dbt, Spark submit, and control-flow types like If/Else and For Each. You define dependencies by setting `depends_on` for each task, creating the DAG. Tasks with no dependency on each other run in parallel automatically. You can pass values between tasks using task values (`dbutils.jobs.taskValues.set` / `.get`), enabling dynamic parameter handoffs across the DAG.
fj: In a Databricks Workflow, tasks always run sequentially in the order you added them to the job.
fs: Databricks Workflow tasks run in parallel when they have no declared dependency on each other. Sequential execution only applies to tasks explicitly connected via `depends_on` in the DAG.

q: What are Databricks Asset Bundles (Declarative Automation Bundles) and how do they support CI/CD for Databricks resources?
a: Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs), now officially called Declarative Automation Bundles, are an infrastructure-as-code (IaC) framework where all Databricks resources—jobs, pipelines, clusters, model serving endpoints, dashboards—are defined as YAML configuration files alongside your source code in version control. You use the Databricks CLI (`databricks bundle deploy`) to deploy bundles to different target environments (dev, staging, prod), with environment-specific variable overrides for things like cluster sizes or catalog names. The bundle structure supports pre/post-deploy hooks, integration with GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps for CI/CD pipelines, and secret resolution at deploy time rather than storing credentials in YAML. In 2026, Databricks deprecated the Terraform-based engine in favor of the Direct Deployment Engine, making bundle deployment faster and reducing the dependency on external IaC tooling.
fj: Databricks Asset Bundles are only useful for deploying notebooks and cannot manage jobs or pipelines.
fs: Declarative Automation Bundles can define and deploy any Databricks resource type including jobs, Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines, clusters, model serving endpoints, SQL warehouses, and dashboards—all as YAML alongside code.

q: How do you configure task failure handling, retries, and alerting in a Databricks Lakeflow Job?
a: At the task level, you can set `max_retries` (how many times to retry a failed task), `retry_on_timeout` (whether a timed-out task counts as a retryable failure), and `min_retry_interval_millis` (backoff between retries). At the job level, you define a `health` rule to mark a job as unhealthy if it exceeds a duration threshold. For alerting, you configure notification settings with email addresses or webhook destinations triggered on job start, success, failure, or when the run is skipped. You can also set `on_failure` at the task level to specify whether a downstream task should still run if its upstream fails (`RUN_IF_ALL_FAILED`, `RUN_IF_AT_LEAST_ONE_FAILED`, `RUN_IF_ALL_SUCCESS`, etc.), enabling cleanup or alerting tasks that execute regardless of upstream failures.
fj: Retries in Databricks Jobs only apply to the entire job run, not to individual tasks.
fs: Retries in Databricks Jobs are configurable at the individual task level, not just the job level. Each task can have its own `max_retries` and retry interval, giving fine-grained control over failure handling.

q: How do run-time parameters and widgets work in parameterized Databricks pipelines, and how do you pass them from a Job to a notebook task?
a: Widgets defined with `dbutils.widgets.text()`, `dbutils.widgets.dropdown()`, etc. create input parameters for a notebook. When a notebook is run as a Job task, the Job passes parameter values into widget bindings under `base_parameters` in the task definition—the widget code then reads them with `dbutils.widgets.get('param_name')`. For multi-task jobs, you can pass computed values between tasks using `dbutils.jobs.taskValues.set(key, value)` in an upstream task and `dbutils.jobs.taskValues.get(taskKey, key)` in a downstream task, enabling dynamic pipeline behavior like passing a partition date from an ingestion task to a transformation task. For Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines, you pass parameters at the pipeline configuration level and read them inside a pipeline with `spark.conf.get('pipelines.parameter_name')`.
fj: Task values set with dbutils.jobs.taskValues are available globally across all job runs, not just the current run.
fs: Task values are scoped to a single job run. They allow data passing between tasks within one run but are not shared across different job runs or jobs.

code.label: Passing task values between tasks
lines: # Task 1: ingest — set a task value
# Task 2: transform — read the task value from Task 1

q: What is Delta Sharing, what are its key use cases, and what are its primary limitations?
a: Delta Sharing is an open protocol for sharing live, read-only data across organizational and cloud boundaries without copying or replicating the data. The provider creates a share (a Unity Catalog securable object containing tables, views, and volumes), defines recipients, and issues a credential token. Recipients can access the shared data using Spark, Pandas, Power BI, or any Delta Sharing-compatible reader. Key use cases include cross-cloud data monetization, sharing data with partners or subsidiaries, enabling a Data Mesh topology across business units, and sharing AI models. Key limitations: shared tables must be in Delta or managed Iceberg format; query result sizes above ~10 GB may cause materialization failures; shared foreign tables do not support predicate pushdown or LIMIT; Delta Sharing uses Unity Catalog credentials and thus requires a UC-enabled workspace on the provider side.
fj: Delta Sharing requires both the provider and recipient to use Databricks.
fs: Delta Sharing is an open protocol. The provider needs a Unity Catalog-enabled Databricks workspace, but recipients can be any Delta Sharing-compatible client including Spark, pandas, Power BI, or a custom implementation using the open Delta Sharing protocol—no Databricks license required for the recipient.

code.label: Create and configure a Delta Share
lines: COMMENT 'Sharing customer analytics with partner org';
-- Add a table to the share
GRANT SELECT ON SHARE customer_data_share TO RECIPIENT partner_org;

q: What is Lakehouse Federation and how do you configure it to query an external PostgreSQL database from Databricks without data movement?
a: Lakehouse Federation is Databricks' query federation platform that lets you run SQL queries against external databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Oracle, Teradata, and more) directly from Databricks through Unity Catalog, with no ETL or data copying required. The configuration has two steps: first, create a Connection—a Unity Catalog securable that stores the credentials and connection string for the external system. Second, create a Foreign Catalog that mirrors the external database's schemas and tables in Unity Catalog. Once set up, you query the foreign tables using standard SQL just like any Delta table; Databricks pushes down predicates and projections to the external system for efficiency. Access is governed by Unity Catalog permissions, giving you consistent access control, lineage, and audit logging across both native and federated data.
fj: Lakehouse Federation copies data from the external database into a Delta table on first query and then caches it for subsequent queries.
fs: Lakehouse Federation does not copy or cache data. Queries execute in real time against the external system with pushdown optimization. The data stays in the source database; only query results are returned to the caller.

code.label: Lakehouse Federation setup for PostgreSQL
lines: -- Step 2: Create a foreign catalog mirroring the external DB
-- Step 3: Query the foreign table directly

q: How do you implement idempotent pipeline patterns in Databricks to ensure safe reruns?
a: Three main patterns ensure idempotency. First, partition overwrite: set `spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode = 'dynamic'` and use INSERT OVERWRITE so that only partitions present in the new data are replaced atomically—reruns produce the same partition state. Second, MERGE INTO: use a business key to match source and target rows, updating or inserting as appropriate. Because MERGE is deterministic on the same input data, running it twice on the same source produces the same target state. Third, Auto Loader / Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines with checkpointing: the checkpoint tracks which files or offsets have been processed, guaranteeing exactly-once processing regardless of retries. For all patterns, avoid pure INSERT INTO (append) without deduplication logic—repeated runs will produce duplicate rows. Also design sources to be replayable (immutable files in object storage or Delta change feeds) so you can re-read the same input safely.
fj: To make a pipeline idempotent, just add a DISTINCT clause to the final SELECT to remove duplicates after each run.
fs: DISTINCT removes duplicates from a query result but does not prevent them from being written to the target on repeated runs. True idempotency requires the write operation itself (INSERT OVERWRITE, MERGE INTO, or streaming checkpoints) to be idempotent, not a post-hoc deduplication.

q: How do you apply DevOps best practices to Databricks projects using Repos and Declarative Automation Bundles?
a: Databricks Repos (Git Folders) integrate the workspace with any Git provider (GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps, Bitbucket). You clone a remote repository into the workspace and can pull, push, branch, and commit directly from the UI or CLI. For CI/CD, the recommended pattern is: developers work in a feature branch; on a pull request, a GitHub Actions (or equivalent) workflow runs `databricks bundle validate` and `databricks bundle deploy --target staging` to deploy to a non-production environment and run integration tests; on merge to main, the workflow runs `databricks bundle deploy --target prod`. Bundle YAML files live alongside notebook and Python source files in the same repo, making infrastructure and code changes atomic. Secrets are stored in Databricks Secret Scopes and referenced in bundle YAML as variables, never hardcoded. Unit tests use `pytest` locally with Databricks Connect or a mocked Spark session.
fj: The correct CI/CD approach is to export notebooks as HTML from the workspace and import them into production manually.
fs: Manual notebook export/import is error-prone, not version-controlled, and does not scale. The correct approach is source-controlled YAML bundles with an automated CI/CD pipeline that deploys resources consistently across environments.

q: How do you configure monitoring and alerting for production data pipelines in Databricks?
a: Databricks provides several layers of production monitoring. For Lakeflow Jobs, you configure notification destinations (email, webhook, Slack via webhook) at the job level triggered on run start, success, failure, or exceeded duration. You can also define job-level health rules that mark a run unhealthy if it exceeds a wall-clock threshold, enabling SLA-based alerting. For Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines, the pipeline event log (a Delta table named `<pipeline_id>.event_log`) records every pipeline update, expectation result, flow status, and error. You query this table to build data quality dashboards or route alerts. System tables in Unity Catalog (e.g., `system.lakeflow.run_timeline`) provide a queryable audit trail of all job and pipeline runs across the workspace, enabling cross-team operational dashboards. Databricks also integrates with external observability stacks via webhook-based alerts or by forwarding system table data to external systems.
fj: You can only monitor Databricks pipelines by manually checking the Jobs UI; there is no programmatic way to set up alerts.
fs: Databricks supports programmatic alerting via notification destinations configured in job YAML or the API, webhook integrations for Slack/PagerDuty, system tables for SQL-based operational dashboards, and event logs for Declarative Pipelines—all without manual UI checks.

q: How do you GRANT and REVOKE permissions at the catalog, schema, and table levels in Unity Catalog?
a: Unity Catalog uses SQL GRANT and REVOKE statements that follow a standard pattern: `GRANT <privilege> ON <securable_type> <securable_name> TO <principal>`. The securable hierarchy is metastore → catalog → schema → table/view/volume/function. Privileges are inherited downward: granting USE CATALOG on a catalog allows the principal to navigate it but not read tables—they also need USE SCHEMA and SELECT on specific objects. To allow full read access to a table you typically grant USE CATALOG, USE SCHEMA, and SELECT. To allow table creation you add CREATE TABLE on the schema. REVOKE uses the same syntax to remove a previously granted privilege. `ALL PRIVILEGES` is a shorthand but should be used carefully in production. The `SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE <name>` statement lists current privileges. Principals can be users (email addresses), service principals, or groups.
fj: Granting SELECT on a catalog automatically grants SELECT on all schemas and tables inside it.
fs: Unity Catalog privileges do not cascade automatically for data access. A principal with SELECT on the catalog still needs USE CATALOG, USE SCHEMA on the specific schema, and SELECT on the specific table. The only exception is that object owners implicitly hold all privileges on their owned object.

code.label: Grant minimal read access to a table
lines: -- Allow a group to navigate the catalog
GRANT USE CATALOG ON CATALOG prod_catalog TO `analysts`;
GRANT USE SCHEMA ON SCHEMA prod_catalog.reporting TO `analysts`;
GRANT SELECT ON TABLE prod_catalog.reporting.daily_revenue TO `analysts`;
REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE prod_catalog.reporting.daily_revenue FROM `analysts`;

q: What are the key Unity Catalog roles — metastore admin, catalog owner, schema owner, table owner — and what can each do?
a: The metastore admin is an account-level role that manages the Unity Catalog metastore itself: creating and deleting catalogs, managing storage credentials, managing external locations, and assigning catalog ownership. There can be only one metastore per region. A catalog owner has all privileges on a catalog and can grant privileges to other principals on that catalog and its children; they are set via `ALTER CATALOG ... SET OWNER TO`. A schema owner has all privileges on the schema and all objects within it, including the ability to create, modify, and drop tables, views, and functions inside the schema. A table owner has all privileges on a specific table, including SELECT, MODIFY, and the ability to grant those privileges to others. Ownership is separate from the MANAGE privilege—owners implicitly have MANAGE and can change ownership. All ownership-based governance supports both users and groups as owners.
fj: The metastore admin automatically has read access to all tables in all catalogs.
fs: The metastore admin role governs metastore-level administration (catalogs, storage, external locations) but does not automatically grant data-level privileges like SELECT on tables. Data access is controlled separately by the object ownership hierarchy and explicit GRANT statements.

q: How do you configure and query Unity Catalog audit logs for compliance and security monitoring in Databricks?
a: Unity Catalog writes structured audit events to system tables in the `system` catalog, which is automatically available in every Unity Catalog-enabled workspace. The primary table is `system.access.audit`, which records every data access event—SELECT, INSERT, MERGE, DROP, GRANT, REVOKE—with timestamps, user identity, IP address, workspace ID, and the specific object accessed. You query these tables using standard SQL in DBSQL or a notebook. For compliance use cases (SOC 2, HIPAA), you can build dashboards that track sensitive table access, failed authentication attempts, and privilege changes. You can also configure Databricks to export audit logs to external SIEM systems (Splunk, Azure Sentinel) via log delivery. The audit log retention in system tables follows the Unity Catalog system table retention policy (typically 1 year by default).
fj: Audit logs in Databricks are only accessible to the account admin via the account console and cannot be queried with SQL.
fs: Unity Catalog audit logs are stored in the `system.access.audit` system table and are queryable with standard SQL by authorized users. This enables self-service compliance reporting and integration with BI tools without relying solely on the account console.

code.label: Query audit logs for sensitive table access

q: What is the difference between Delta Sharing and Lakehouse Federation, and when would you use each?
a: Delta Sharing is an outbound sharing protocol: the Databricks provider pushes read access to Delta tables out to external consumers (partners, other clouds, non-Databricks clients) without copying data. The consumer reads live data using Delta Sharing-compatible clients. Lakehouse Federation is an inbound query federation capability: Databricks connects to external databases (PostgreSQL, Snowflake, Redshift, BigQuery, etc.) and queries their data in place without ingesting it. Use Delta Sharing when you own the data and want to share it externally with zero data movement. Use Lakehouse Federation when you need to join or query data that lives in a system you do not own and cannot or do not want to replicate into the Lakehouse. Both features avoid data duplication, but they differ in direction of data access and in the systems they connect.
fj: Delta Sharing and Lakehouse Federation both copy data into a Delta table in the Databricks workspace before making it queryable.
fs: Neither feature copies data. Delta Sharing provides direct read access to files in the provider's object storage via a credential token. Lakehouse Federation executes queries in real time against the source database and returns results. No persistent copies are created in either case.

q: How do you configure Lakehouse Federation for a Senior-level use case involving cross-cloud data governance?
a: In a multi-cloud or cross-system architecture, Lakehouse Federation lets you register connections to external systems (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) in Unity Catalog and expose them as foreign catalogs. The key governance benefit is that Unity Catalog enforces access control uniformly: you use the same GRANT/REVOKE syntax on foreign tables as on native Delta tables, and access events appear in the same `system.access.audit` table. For a cross-cloud scenario, you might create a connection to BigQuery for a Google Cloud team and a connection to Redshift for an AWS team, both accessible from the same Databricks workspace with unified lineage. The gotcha for Staff/Sr candidates is that Lakehouse Federation is read-only—you cannot write back to the external system through the federation layer. Also, query performance depends on the external system's responsiveness and the quality of predicate pushdown support for that connector.
fj: You can use Lakehouse Federation to write transformed data back to the source PostgreSQL database after processing it in Databricks.
fs: Lakehouse Federation is read-only. You can query external data in place but cannot write back through the federation layer. If you need to write to an external system, you must use a JDBC write operation directly, not through the foreign catalog abstraction.

q: How do you apply table constraints (NOT NULL, CHECK, primary key, foreign key) in Delta Lake, and are they enforced?
a: Delta Lake on Databricks supports four types of constraints. NOT NULL is enforced: any insert or update that tries to write a NULL into a NOT NULL column is rejected. CHECK constraints define a boolean expression that every row must satisfy and are also enforced—violating rows are rejected at write time, and if you add a CHECK constraint to an existing table with existing violations, the ALTER TABLE command fails. Primary key and foreign key constraints are informational (not enforced by default): they exist to convey semantic meaning and enable query optimizer hints (Databricks can exploit them for join reordering if RELY is specified), but they do not block writes of duplicate or orphaned values. All constraints are added with `ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT` and dropped with `ALTER TABLE ... DROP CONSTRAINT`. Primary and foreign key constraints require Unity Catalog (not available in `hive_metastore`).
fj: Primary key constraints in Delta Lake are fully enforced, preventing duplicate rows just like in a traditional RDBMS.
fs: Primary key constraints in Delta Lake are informational by default (they are not enforced at write time). They exist for documentation and query optimization purposes. Only NOT NULL and CHECK constraints are enforced and will reject violating writes.

code.label: Adding constraints to a Delta table
lines: ALTER COLUMN order_id SET NOT NULL;

q: What are the core components of a Databricks workspace and how do they relate to each other?
a: A Databricks workspace is a cloud-based collaborative environment that groups all Databricks assets under a single URL. The core components are: Notebooks—web-based code editors supporting Python, SQL, Scala, and R that run on attached clusters; Clusters—sets of VMs (all-purpose for interactive work, job clusters for scheduled runs) that provide the Spark compute engine; DBFS (Databricks File System)—an abstraction layer over cloud object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS) that provides a filesystem interface, though Databricks recommends storing production data in cloud storage accessed via Unity Catalog external locations rather than DBFS root; Repos (Git Folders)—workspace folders synced to a remote Git repository, enabling version control; Workflows—the job scheduling and orchestration service (Lakeflow Jobs); Secrets—encrypted key-value pairs for credentials stored in secret scopes; and Unity Catalog—the governance layer that provides the three-level namespace (catalog.schema.table) visible across all workspaces in an account.
fj: DBFS is a separate distributed file system independent of cloud storage that persists data permanently on Databricks-managed disks.
fs: DBFS is an abstraction layer over cloud object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS). It does not have its own persistent storage—data written to DBFS paths is actually stored in your cloud storage account. Databricks discourages storing production data in the DBFS root for security and governance reasons.

q: How do you navigate the Databricks workspace UI for common data engineering tasks such as running notebooks, managing clusters, and monitoring jobs?
a: The Databricks workspace UI is organized around a left navigation panel with icons for key areas. The Workspace browser lets you organize notebooks and files into folders. The Compute section (formerly Clusters) shows all-purpose and job clusters with their state, size, and runtime version; you create a new cluster here or attach a notebook to an existing one. The Workflows section (Lakeflow Jobs) shows all jobs, their schedules, last run status, and run history with task-level logs. The Data section provides the Unity Catalog browser to navigate catalogs, schemas, tables, and volumes, and run data previews. The Pipelines section lists all Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines with visual DAG views and event logs. The SQL Editor gives an interactive SQL interface backed by Databricks SQL warehouses. For troubleshooting, the Spark UI is accessible from the cluster detail page, and job task logs are accessible from the run detail page.
fj: To see the execution plan of a Spark job, you must SSH into the driver node and access the Spark history server.
fs: The Spark UI is accessible directly from the cluster detail page in the Databricks workspace UI under the Spark UI tab—no SSH required. From there you can inspect DAGs, stage details, and task metrics for both interactive notebook runs and job runs.

q: How do you deploy a Lakeflow Job with multiple task types including a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline task in a production workflow?
a: A production Lakeflow Job that includes a Declarative Pipelines task combines several task types in a DAG. A typical pattern: Task 1 is a Python script or notebook that performs pre-checks or raw data landing validation; Task 2 is a Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines task that triggers a named pipeline (the pipeline processes bronze-to-silver transformations); Task 3 is a SQL task that runs post-pipeline data quality checks using DBSQL; Task 4 is a Python wheel task that triggers downstream model retraining or metric computation. Task 2 depends on Task 1, and Tasks 3 and 4 both depend on Task 2. When the pipeline task runs, it executes the Declarative Pipeline in its configured mode (triggered) and waits for it to complete before marking the task as succeeded or failed. This pattern is fully expressible in Declarative Automation Bundles YAML.
fj: A Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline can only be triggered manually from the Pipelines UI and cannot be called from a Lakeflow Job.
fs: Lakeflow Jobs support a dedicated pipeline task type that triggers a named Declarative Pipeline as part of a job DAG, waits for pipeline completion, and propagates success or failure to downstream tasks. This is the standard way to orchestrate pipeline execution in production.

q: What is the interoperability model between Delta Sharing and Lakehouse Federation for building a data mesh on Databricks?
a: In a data mesh architecture on Databricks, Delta Sharing and Lakehouse Federation play complementary roles. Domain teams publish their canonical datasets via Delta Sharing: they create shares backed by Unity Catalog-governed Delta tables and issue credentials to consuming domains. This maintains domain ownership—the producing team's Unity Catalog enforces access, and consumers read live data without copies. Lakehouse Federation handles the reverse: when a domain needs to read from a non-Databricks system (say, a PostgreSQL OLTP database or a Snowflake warehouse), it registers a connection and foreign catalog in its own Unity Catalog and queries the external data in-place. The mesh control plane sits in Unity Catalog: all data products (shared tables, foreign tables, native Delta tables) are discoverable from the catalog browser, subject to the same GRANT/REVOKE model, and included in system audit logs. This unified governance is the key differentiator of the Databricks data mesh approach.
fj: Delta Sharing and Lakehouse Federation are redundant features; you should pick one and use it consistently.
fs: The two features have distinct directionality and use cases. Delta Sharing is for sharing data you own outward to consumers. Lakehouse Federation is for reading external data you do not own into your workspace. They are complementary in a data mesh: Delta Sharing for outbound data products, Lakehouse Federation for inbound external system access.

q: How do you grant permissions on a Delta Share to a recipient and what does a recipient need to start querying the shared data?
a: Granting access to a Delta Share involves three steps. First, create a share object in Unity Catalog and add tables, views, or volumes to it. Second, create a recipient—either a Unity Catalog recipient (for Databricks-to-Databricks sharing, using metastore-to-metastore identity) or an open-protocol recipient (using a token-based credential file). Third, grant the share to the recipient: `GRANT SELECT ON SHARE <share_name> TO RECIPIENT <recipient_name>`. For Databricks-to-Databricks sharing, the recipient's metastore admin creates a foreign catalog from the share using the sharing identifier—no credential file is needed. For open-protocol recipients, they download an `activation_link` credential file and use it in their Spark, Pandas, or Power BI client. The recipient can only read the exact tables and columns included in the share—they have no access to the underlying storage paths or other tables in the provider's catalog.
fj: Once you grant a Delta Share to a recipient, they automatically gain access to all tables in the provider's catalog.
fs: A recipient only sees the tables and views explicitly added to the share object. They have no visibility into other tables, schemas, or catalogs in the provider's Unity Catalog, and they cannot navigate the provider's storage directly.

code.label: Full Delta Sharing setup (provider side)
lines: CREATE SHARE sales_share COMMENT 'Sharing Q2 sales with partner';
ALTER SHARE sales_share ADD TABLE prod_catalog.gold.q2_revenue;
GRANT SELECT ON SHARE sales_share TO RECIPIENT partner_metastore;

q: How do you define and use Databricks Workflows task failure conditions — RUN_IF_ALL_SUCCESS, RUN_IF_AT_LEAST_ONE_FAILED — to build resilient pipelines?
a: Databricks Lakeflow Jobs let you set run conditions on each task to control execution flow based on upstream task outcomes. `RUN_IF_ALL_SUCCESS` (the default) means the task only runs if all upstream dependencies succeeded—this is standard for transformation and output tasks. `RUN_IF_AT_LEAST_ONE_FAILED` is used for tasks that should only run when something went wrong, such as a cleanup task, an incident notification, or a rollback step. `RUN_IF_ALL_FAILED` requires every upstream task to have failed. `RUN_IF_AT_LEAST_ONE_SUCCESS` allows partial success to trigger a task. `ALL_DONE` runs the task regardless of upstream outcome—ideal for mandatory cleanup or audit-logging tasks that must always execute. By combining these conditions, you can build pipelines that self-heal: e.g., a cleanup notebook that only triggers if the transformation task failed, followed by an alerting task that always runs to report the final status.
fj: If any task in a Databricks Job fails, the entire job halts immediately and no further tasks run.
fs: Databricks Jobs continue executing downstream tasks if their run condition is met. For example, a task with `RUN_IF_AT_LEAST_ONE_FAILED` will execute even if an upstream task failed. Only tasks with `RUN_IF_ALL_SUCCESS` (the default) are blocked by upstream failures.

q: What steps are required to set up a Databricks Asset Bundle for a CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions targeting prod?
a: The setup has four phases. Phase 1: scaffold the bundle with `databricks bundle init` (or manually create `databricks.yml`) at the repo root, defining resources (jobs, pipelines), variables, and targets (dev, staging, prod). Phase 2: configure authentication—use a Databricks service principal with OAuth or a PAT token, stored as GitHub Actions secrets, never in YAML. Reference secrets as bundle variables resolved at deploy time. Phase 3: define the GitHub Actions workflow with two jobs: a PR job that runs `databricks bundle validate && databricks bundle deploy --target staging` and runs integration tests against the staging workspace; a main-merge job that runs `databricks bundle deploy --target prod --var catalog=prod_catalog` for production deployment. Phase 4: define environment-specific variable overrides in the `targets` section of `databricks.yml` so that catalog names, cluster sizes, and schedule frequencies differ between dev, staging, and prod without duplicating configuration.
fj: You should store production Databricks PAT tokens directly in databricks.yml so the CI pipeline can authenticate automatically.
fs: PAT tokens must never be stored in YAML files in source control. Store them as encrypted secrets in GitHub Actions (or your CI provider's secret store) and inject them as environment variables at runtime. Databricks recommends OAuth M2M authentication with service principals for CI/CD over PAT tokens.
